sentences.text: No.
No.
Those sausages is as good as gold.
You see, when you-- you divide it quick, you change it into other things, Like vodka, bread, butter, main things.
Good soap, things like that you can buy for that.
Even a slice like that buys it for you, in different-- in a different direction.
That means you can do business.
That's what I did to survive.
And then I'm able to give my ration to somebody I like or a friend of mine.
My soup I never, you know, hardly ate it.
Oh, a few times, not any particular time.
But a few times it happened.
And then I went-- I had to go out, because it became too suspicious.
And I went into-- and I went to the Union, what you call, factory, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] which were making-- ammunition factory of the camp.
There were thousands of people working there.
Yeah.
It was in 1943.
I was there for-- for eight months.
Yes.
Yes, it did.
We had a kapo called Schultz. oberkapo.
He was a Jew, a Yugoslav or Croatian Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was very bad.
If you were on the right side-- but he was schizophrenia.

You know, he could be good and then-- most of the people in the camp were schizophrenia anyhow, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] including the high SS were schizophrenic, because they adopted Jewish children from the-- from the-- they didn't let them go to the gas chambers.
They used them as pets, cats and dogs, like playing with their children.
When they got tired of them, they put them in the gas chambers and got new ones.
They played in their homes.
Yeah.
Yes, I did, because a couple of times I was cleaning-- I was selected to clean or deliver laundry in the later stage to them in their homes, like a laundryman, so I saw that.
I saw the Jewish children playing there, beautiful dressed and all.
And then I went to the Union.
We worked in ammunition factory together with women.
We had connection with women.
So a lot of men wanted to have that connection with women and they paid me for it.
For delivering letters and notes and finding out which women are with me, or-- some women which are working there are working with their wives in-- in Birkenau.
They had to pay me in bread or in rations or in cigarettes, mostly cigarettes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Mostly I took cigarettes.
They could-- they could change it themself into cigarettes and pay me in cigarettes.
Cigarettes was a currency.
Yeah.
I risked my life.
I risked something.
If they find notes for me from somebody else, I get 10 and 15 on-- on my back.
Nothing is without risks here.
And why should I do it for strangers?
In the factory itself, pretty good.
They were-- because the SS who were [INAUDIBLE] were specialists like engineers and things like that.
They put them in there.
They were in the SS uniform, but they were engineers and turner and fitters and things like that.
They were professional people.
They were not animals from the bush, you know.
So they treated the prisoners like a-- like a professional, like their apprentice or something.
And then there were overseers, which were not professionals.
They were in charge of the whole group of prisoners.
Well, not so good.
Screaming, shouting, pushing, usual thing.
Oh, hitting, yeah, sometimes.
But it was in general much better than the outside, much better.
On the machine I was on a lathe, turning, turning and fitting on a lathe.
And then the women were packing.
And it was really factory work, you know.
And-- and--
Ammunition.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And in that particular time [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there were three women-- that was already towards the end of '44-- three women who were arrested.
They were delivering dynamite to the camp, to the men.
And in that particular time, Crematorium 4 was blown up.
The SS was shot.
The scharfuhrer who was in charge of Crematorium 4 was thrown in the furnace by the Sonderkommando.
Sonderkommando people which are working in the crematorium.
Jewish prisoners.
They get whatever they want.
They can drink.
They can eat.
They're all strong.
They changed them every six months.
They killed them and then put new ones in.
And in end of '44, we saw a big fire in Auschwitz [INAUDIBLE] lager, on the horizon, big fire, you know, like red.
The sky was red.
And the Crematorium 4 was burning.
Because Zygmund told me.
The fire brigade went out from our camp.
The whole fire brigade with fire trucks and everything went out there.
From Zygmund, from the fire brigades.
From the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were 500 people escaping, Sonderkommando. 480 people escaped.
Jews.
Sonderkommando.
In the-- in the forest.
Yeah.
But they got caught.
All of them, unfortunately, with dogs and everything.
They couldn't go far.
They were sending out thousands of troops after them.
I think there were three or four.
I don't know exactly.
They were hanged in Birkenau.
They hung them up.
They provided the ammunition from Union, where I was working.
They provided to blow up the-- blow up the furnaces and blow up the tower.
Yeah.
They were-- they were shifting explosives.
That's right, and supplied it to the man.
And the man smuggled it to Sonderkommado.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's how the whole furnaces were blown up, and that the watchtowers were blown up.
They put ammunition under the watchtower with machine guns and it flew up in the air.
No.
They were hanged in Birkenau.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were hanged in Birkenau.
They were-- they were in Block-- they were in Block 11.
They were all in Block 11, but they were transported to Birkenau to be hung.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Slovakians, they were, three young women.
Unfortunately, there's no memorial at the moment for them at all saying that they were Jewish women.
Anyhow, I worked in Union for quite a while.
And then I got-- there was not enough-- there was enough to-- business to do, you know.
It was a factory.
So I got the hint from somebody there was a vacation, through my connections which I made in the meantime.
I was already older prisoner.
I was already older survivor.
And I got into a SS laundry.
That was the best job I ever had.
It was under the roof.
It was warm.
It was clean.
My uniform was clean.
Everything was picked.
And I could-- I had the freedom of moving around.

Delivering the law only for this obersturmfuhrer, this unterscharfuhrer, this, this, this, and everybody was giving me a few cigarettes and this, and good done and well done, can you do my shirt, et cetera, my pants.
And I was-- there were about only 10 people working there.
The whole kommando was 10 people.
And I was amongst the 10.
Not all, no.
Four Jews.
The rest was not Jews.
The foreman was not Jews.
There was no beating.
There was nothing.
[LAUGHS] I saw beatings every day.
I saw beatings every day and I saw hangings everyday.
Every morning.
Some of them were Serb partisans, Serbs, with the-- you know?
And some of them were people who tried to escape.
I was-- they put a board around them.
I-- I-- I was trying to escape.
And they hang them up.
And some of them were-- that means local prisoners, Poles mostly, not Jews, Poles.
And some of them were Serb partisans, they caught them.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] some of them were punished prisoners.
No.
They killed them.
They didn't have to hang them.
They killed them in the camp.
They didn't have to string them up.
As a matter of fact, some of the people they hanged-- there was a fellow, as I said to you, a [INAUDIBLE] called Tadek.
Tadek was a Polnish, fellow, very good looking, tall.
He was in love with a Jewish girl, a Slovakian girl, in Birkenau.
He fell in lover with here.
They had something together.
He stole a-- he had free access to go anywhere.
He stole a [INAUDIBLE].
He stole a uniform.
He took her and he drove away, to Krakow.
They caught him and her.
Both got hung in front of everybody.
Yeah. '43.
End of '43.
Yeah.
We all saw it.
The hanging was taking place in the appell in the morning.
Was prepared a table and on that table was chairs.
And they stood on the chairs with a noose around us the thing-- around the structure.
And then the chair-- the chairs were kicked away by the SS.
And they floated.
They dangled.
That was it.
No-- no-- no-- no hood.
No nothing.
And the most courageous people I have ever seen hung was the Serbs.
They spit in the Germans' faces and said, we'll get you.
You won't survive that war.
We'll get you.
They stood up.
And as a matter of fact, I heard from other people-- I don't care what they say here now about the Serbs.
I got great respect for the Serbs, because the Serbs were the only one who were interfering with the transports.
They stopped two or three transports.
They killed all the Germans on top of the guards and they opened the doors and they took the Jews with them in the mountains.
From people who had been taken in the mountains.
In-- in displaced persons camps in Germany [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] after the war.

Look, anybody who was in Auschwitz and he-- he had plenty-- he saw further than just his barrack would witness brutalities all the time, particular by-- by-- by Germans, by German kapos, by oberkapos, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] by Polnish-- half-Polnish and half-Germans, which are from Schleswig, you know, Poznania.
Kick them, mistreat them, beat them.
Because they were sadists and the-- and those people were helpless and weak.
And they wanted to make a good eye in the Germans' eyes.
They want to show themself.
Germans had to stop prisoner kapos to-- there was more people killed by prisoners than there were by Germans.
Inmates.
Croatians, Germans, Ukrainians.
No.
No.
You see, in Auschwitz, we hardly had any seniority in Jewish-- all the Jewish kapos and things like that was all happening in Birkenau [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] or in the Gypsy camps.
But in-- in Auschwitz, there was no-- no seniority of Jews at all.
They were all that nationality, or German criminals which were in charge, like oberkapos, green-- green-- you see, there was a difference between green triangle and red triangle.
The red triangle were political, where the green triangle were criminals.
I was sleeping in a very good block, with people which had already good kommandos.
We call them kommandos, working kommandos.
We all had our little wardrobe even, our little cupboards, where we can lock in our food and our personal belongings.
We didn't have to keep it under the mattress, like in other barracks.
And we went to work at-- after the-- after the roll call at 6 o'clock, we went to work.
Went to work.
We had a-- a [INAUDIBLE] which is-- which is a role who irons the clothing.
We had washing troughs.
We boiled the laundry in copper-- in copper-- big copper kettles with wooden sticks.
We had soap.
We had everything in there, at work.
We had drying cupboards and drying rooms where we're drying the-- the laundry.
And we are working exclusively for the SS administration and SS in charge.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Not by myself.

I always had somebody to deliver and I had a guard behind me, outside every time you go outside the gates, you had to report and things were written, time-- the time and who passes and what number passes and where you're going.
And then when you come in, you had to be in at a certain time.
Well, the SS barracks, they were outside the camp.
That's the barracks, outside the camp.
If you go further in the camp, we call it the [NON-ENGLISH] was 50 kilometers.
There was a second-- a second security guards.
Yeah.
Yeah.
But not delivering laundry.
That's when I went first, when-- with the first kommandos we went outside the 50 kilometers.
And we saw civilian workers.
We saw civilian Polnish workers working in the fields.
And they were not far.
Some of the kommandos, they worked together with civilian Polnish workers, but all the time under guards and dogs, naturally.
And and then-- and then after-- after 50 kilometers, you're more or less outside, you know.
And I delivered the laundry and things like that to the SS houses, which were like a small house.
And the staff, they had their staff there, their woman whatever.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
I also had SS guards which I deal with on the black market.
Well, I-- they-- how I deal with them.
I gave them cigarettes.
They gave me vodka.
Well, from my connections.
[LAUGHS]
Well, I told you, I-- about the things I did and risked my life on the line.
And once I got caught and I got punished.
They hung me up and they beat 10 on my back with a-- with a oxtail.
Oh, on cups and hang me up on chains on the ceiling and they swung me.
And there was one fellow standing there, oberkapo, and he hit me.
No.
But I know the fellow who stood by and counting.
Unterscharfuhrer Moll.
He had a pig farm once, a long time ago.
Moll, he was known.
He said, if you don't know me, you'll know me.
That was his speech.
I went to different blocks to talk to people.
I made my connections.
Oh, yeah.
You can-- you can go.
You can go anywhere you like.
Yeah, until a certain time.
Until 10 o'clock.
Yeah.
Entertainment.
No.
There was no entertainment.
A bit of singing, a bit of this, a bit of that, that's about it.
There was not much entertainment.
The only entertainment that the-- the SS got, you see.
If they get a boxer like Saunders and they put another one against him and they were all sitting around.
And they let them thresh it out and they were-- that's the way they were entertaining themselves.
It was a friend of mine.
He was in the [INAUDIBLE].
He was champion of Holland, a boxer.
That's the one who survived.
He's in Curacao.
Boxing.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
They put even SS against him.
He beat them up in the ring.
Not very-- they laughed.
[LAUGHS]
No.
You're asking me about atrocities.
I saw atrocities.
I saw Orthodox Jews.
They put them into thick coats, into ladies' fur coats.
They put rope around them and they put them thick Russian gloves on and Russian caps on.
And they put Dobermans on them and they torn them apart.
In the camp.
Oh, I don't know when. '42, '43.
And they were laughing.
That was the SS entertainment.
Oh, yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
I was doing [NON-ENGLISH].
That means cleaning the barracks.
I was doing that.
And sometime-- once I was helping in the-- in the hospitals.
I was doing that, helping a bit and carrying a bit and doing things.
And other work I did in the camp, I was also in the [NON-ENGLISH], I remember now.
That means in the-- it's called-- you know, to help the waterworks.
Oh, dig-- dig-- dig channels and put pipes in.
We did that too.
And once I had a bit of bad luck.
I struck one of those [NON-ENGLISH] and he was completely drunk. he got hold of us, two guards.
That was before, in the beginning.
And I started to dig.
He said, make a hole.
We dug a hole and there were four of us.
And we dug hole.
But-- and then I realized what I was doing.
I was digging my own grave.
And he was so drunk.
And he said he wanted to shoot us in the grave and said, a German bullet is too good for you [INAUDIBLE] Jews.
And he put it back and walked away.
A German bullet is not-- is too good for you.
The four of us stood there.
Huh?
No.
No.
And the-- and the-- and the infamous one I met was a fellow called Pohl, P-O-H-L.
He was in charge, second to Hitler, administration and economics of the SS.
He ran the Politische Abteilung in Auschwitz.
He was often there.
He was tried in the Nuremberg trial and he was hung.
In Auschwitz?
Oh, he came to the SS laundry and I delivered-- I delivered to his house.
Oh, no.
Yeah.
[INAUDIBLE] I was sorting-- sorting from people who were gassed-- I was sorting their belongings, their clothes and their things like that.
And we were disinfecting it.
The Zyklon B was thrown in-- and the same gas they killed the-- killed the people with.
And after about 15, 20 minutes, they put the fans on and we were going in with gas masks to remove the clothes.
The clothes were sorted.
You know, blouse by blouse and suit by suit and this.
And they were sent to Germany, to the Winterhilfe.
You know what the Winterhilfe is?
To help poor people in Germany or bombed-out people with clothing.
There also-- also in about I think it was also '43.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
Everything was before the laundry.
Yes.
The glasses were sorted, the eye-- the thing.
And then, you know-- and they came to supervise it and was counted and registered.
Oh, the Germans are very efficient with registering and counting and things like that, very efficient, like computers.
We got the news about Stalingrad from the Germans.
They talked.
Not the Germans talked.
Not the Germans talked.
Those idiots they hired them.
They were Ukrainians.
They were Ukrainians who used to be in the Russian army and the Soviet army.
And they-- there-- there was a general called Vlasov.
And he took all the men out of the prisoner camps, all the Ukrainians and things like that.
And he formed a SS battalion, which was authorized by the German government.
And they were guards in the camp.
And the Croatians were guards in the camp.
And the Estonians were guards in the camp, and the Lithuanians and Latvians were guards in the camp.
From them.
From them.
We-- we all knew about that.
There was a underground in Auschwitz.
There was a underground.
Well, I can only tell you what I know, because each cell didn't know from another cell.
It was organized in such a way that if they torture somebody, he only can give away two people.
Well, I was affiliated.
To the people-- to the people who were in there.
It's to gather information, pass them on very slowly to the Jews.
Very carefully [? to whom. ?]
Nothing, just-- just carrying information.
Nothing active.
Nothing with ammunition.
Nothing with escaping.
I was not in escape committee.
The escape committee was organized by Poles, by sophisticated Poles.
No.
No.
No.
Now you see, in-- even if a Jew escapes from Auschwitz, he would not get far, because of the anti-Semitic atmosphere outside the camp.
They would have been given away.
